preamble: The diphthong /ai/ has undergone a notable phonetic transformation in the historical development of East Baltic languages, specifically Lithuanian and Latvian. This shift typically involves a two-step process: an initial monophthongization of /ai/ to an intermediate monophthong, followed by a subsequent diphthongization of this monophthong into /ie/.
phoneticEffects: This is a complex vowel shift involving both monophthongization and diphthongization

examples.note: Monophthongization to an intermediate vowel, then diphthongization to /ie/ in stressed positions.
The PIE root for 'one' with a diphthong (related to *ai or *oi) developed into Lithuanian 'vienas' with /ie/.
The Proto-Baltic word for 'god' with *ei (often grouped with *ai in these shifts) developed into Lithuanian 'dievas' with /ie/.
A similar development to Lithuanian, where Common Baltic *ai shifted to /ie/.
The Proto-Baltic word for 'god' with *ei (often grouped with *ai in these shifts) developed into Latvian 'dievas' with /ie/.

sources: Yamazaki, Y. (2014). Historical laws of Baltic accentuation. In P. Arkadiev, A. Holvoet, & B. Wiemer (Eds.), Baltic Linguistics (pp. 115-138). Language Science Press.
Jasanoff, J. H. (2004). SAUSSURE'S LAW, THE NOM. PL. IN -ai, AND THE TREATMENT OF ACUTE DIPHTHONG. Harvard Working Papers in Linguistics, 9, 1-20.
Levin, J. (1975). The passage of Common Baltic *ë(< *ej and *ai[?]) to ie. (Cited in Jégers, R. (1976). Jégers: JANIS REITERS TULKOJUMA PARAUGS (Book Review). ProQuest.)
